summary: Stancil argues that “self‑serve analytics is a mirage” has become a fashionable shibboleth, but the real issue is that analysts project their own needs onto business users. When you define self‑serve narrowly—mostly as consistent metric extraction—it becomes solvable, while deeper analysis still requires hiring more analytical thinkers.
money_quotes: Self-serve analytics, which promises to open up vast troves of “insight” by making data accessible to everyone at a company, doesn’t actually work.
The problem self-serve is meant to solve—that there are more questions than analysts, and we don’t have time to answer them all—is still a problem, even if self-serve is a lousy solution.
He showed me that my reflexive dismissal of self-serve was wrong because my definition of self-serve was wrong.

They want to choose from a list of understood KPIs, apply it to a filtered set of records, and aggregate it by a particular dimension. It's analytical Mad Libs—show me average order size for orders that used gift cards by month.
If you want to make great decisions with data, you...buy great self-serve tools? No, you hire great analytical thinkers.
key_insight: Self‑serve fails when treated as analyst‑grade exploration, but works when scoped to standardized metric extraction, while truly complex questions still demand more analysts.
